                                                                  FY 2025          Price          Program          FY 2026          Price          Program         FY 2027
 Appropriations Summary                                           Actuals         Change          Change           Enacted         Change          Change          Request

 Operation and Maintenance, Army                                  62,402.0            953.5         -5,820.2       57,535.3            771.6         5,807.8        64,114.7

Description of Operations Financed:

The Operation and Maintenance, Army (OMA) appropriation resources the day-to-day activities of global Army organizations, promoting current, near-term, and strategic
readiness. The FY 2027 budget request directly implements key Defense priorities to rebuild the military, reestablish deterrence, and restore the warrior ethos, while
continuing reform and optimization efforts. This funding is critical for transforming the Army into a leaner, more lethal force aligned with the 2026 National Defense
Strategy.

OMA funds the recruiting, organizing, sustaining, equipping, and training of the All-Volunteer Force for global land combat operations in support of Geographic Combatant
Commands. More specifically, the appropriation funds the Army's Ground and Air Operating Tempo, flying hours, fuel, supplies, and the maintenance of weapons systems
and aircraft. It funds quality of life activities, specialized services for Soldiers, civilians, and their Families, and educational and career development programs for Soldiers
and civilians. OMA funds the Army's management structure, logistics, command, control, and communication programs; the Department of War's contribution to the North
Atlantic Treaty Organization; and Army Combatant Command Support Agency (CCSA) responsibilities.

This request supports a FY 2027 Total Army strength of 972,300 Soldiers across the three components (Active, Army National Guard, and Army Reserve). OMA funds all
training, readiness, and support for 469,000 active component Soldiers and funds select activities for the Army Reserve and the Army National Guard. Additionally, the
OMA FY 2027 request funds 89,634 civilian full-time equivalents (FTEs) providing service-wide support across multiple essential functions.

The FY 2027 OMA budget request of $64,115 million includes $772 million in inflation-driven price increases and results in a net program increase of $5,808 million.
This growth reflects crucial investments in support of the National Defense Strategy and the Army's Continuous Transformation initiatives. The increase also includes core
readiness and quality of life requirements shifting from mandatory funding in FY 2026 to discretionary funding in FY 2027.

Overall Assessment:

Army forces preserve peace through strength and must be prepared to prevail in a complex security environment where strategic competition from revisionist powers is the
predominant threat to our national security interests. OMA resources provide the current and future readiness that enables our Ground Forces to meet those rigorous
demands. The Army is supporting Combatant Commanders around the world today in deterring aggression and assuring our allies.

Army Continuous Transformation: Army Continuous Transformation: The U.S. Army continues to implement and refine its Continuous Transformation strategy, a
comprehensive effort to build a more lethal, resilient, and globally responsive force for 2030 and beyond. This transformation is not a future concept; it is happening now.
By optimizing resources and divesting legacy systems, the Army is generating the trade-space to invest in the key warfighting capabilities required by the 2026 National
Defense Strategy.

                                                                                                            Exhibit PBA-19 Introductory Statement (Appropriation Highlights)

                                                                         DEPARTMENT OF THE ARMY

                                                                Introductory Statement (Appropriation Highlights)
                                                                                   ($ In Millions)



Key initiatives funded in the FY 2027 OMA request include:

Force Structure Realignment: Continuing the reorganization of our formations to create greater combat power and lethality. This includes the evolution of Infantry Brigade
Combat Teams (IBCTs) and the establishment of additional Multi-Domain Task Forces (MDTFs), which are purpose-built to penetrate and disintegrate enemy anti-
access/area denial (A2/AD) networks.

Manning the Transformed Force: Rebuilding the Army’s end strength is a foundational element of transformation. This budget request grows the force to better man our
formations and reduces manning shortfalls in critical specialties. The OMA appropriation is essential to this effort, as it funds the recruiting, training, and sustainment of
these Soldiers, ensuring they are ready to contribute to a more capable and lethal Army.

Optimizing the Enterprise: Streamlining processes and headquarters to improve the speed and effectiveness of decision-making and resource allocation, ensuring the
Army remains an agile and responsible steward of taxpayer dollars. In total, the OMA request includes $-531 million for efficiencies, in the areas of contract services,
printing & reproduction, supplies & materials, travel, and the civilian workforce.

The FY 2027 request represents a deliberate, phased approach to transformation. It ensures that while we build the Army of tomorrow, we remain the most lethal and
ready land force in the world today, prepared to meet any challenge and defend the Nation's interests.

Global Deterrence and Homeland Defense: In FY 2027, the Army's OMA request directly supports the 2026 National Defense Strategy's focus on campaigning and
integrated deterrence. This budget prioritizes the Indo-Pacific Theater by increasing funding for the Pacific Deterrence Initiative (PDI) to $1,565 million. Concurrently, the
request sustains critical U.S. military operations, force protection, and deterrence activities in the U.S. Central Command and U.S. European Command Areas of
Responsibility. These operations are vital for campaigning, strengthening alliances, and ensuring the global force projection capabilities of the Joint Force. The table below
shows PDI funding by Line of Effort:

                                                                  PDI Line of Effort              FY 2025   FY 2026 FY 2027
                                                   Modernized and Strengthened Presence                18.8      12.9    14.4
                                                   Improved Logistics & Prepositioning of Eqpt        108.4      85.7    86.3
                                                   Building Capabilities of Allies and Partners         0.4      47.7    47.1
                                                   Exercises, Training, and Experimentation           420.1     442.0 1,215.2
                                                   Infrastructure Improvements                         18.3      16.5   193.9
                                                   Improve Capabilities for USINDOPACOM                 0.0       7.1     7.6
                                                                      Total PDI                       566.0     611.8 1,564.5

                                                               Introductory Statement (Appropriation Highlights)
                                                                                  ($ In Millions)



Supporting Combatant Command Priorities: This budget request directly resources the day-to-day activities and forward presence of Army forces assigned to Geographic
Combatant Commands (GCC). OMA funding is the primary enabler for persistent campaigning, allowing Army units to participate in joint and multinational exercises,
security cooperation events, and partnership-building activities. These engagements increase interoperability with our allies and partners, enhance regional stability, and
signal U.S. commitment and resolve, which are foundational to integrated deterrence. This request includes significant funding increases to resource these priorities, the
details of which are described within the appropriate sections of this volume.

Homeland Defense: The OMA appropriation is fundamental to the Army’s ability to defend the homeland and provide defense support to civil authorities under U.S.
Northern Command (USNORTHCOM). This request funds the readiness of units designated for homeland defense missions, including response to natural disasters,
domestic emergencies, and consequence management operations. It also funds the deployment, training, and equipping of units mobilized to operationalize and
proactively deter threats to National Defense Areas in accordance with Executive Order 14167, Clarifying the Military's Role in Protecting the Territorial Integrity of the
United States.

Expansion of Army Prepositioned Stocks: The FY 2027 OMA request makes significant investments in the Total Army Power Projection Equipment (TAP2E) initiative. This
strategy enhances global deterrence and responsiveness by positioning combat-ready equipment sets, medical sustainment stocks, and munitions in key strategic regions.
Specifically, FY 2027 expands capability in the Indo-Pacific, by establishing Army Prepositioned Stocks-8 (APS-8) in the Southwest Indo-Pacific and increasing APS-5
(Southwest Asia).

This Operation and Maintenance, Army budget request makes the critical investments needed to ensure the Army can answer the Nation’s call. It provides for our Soldiers,
Civilians, and their Families; it builds readiness for today’s challenges; and it resources the transformation required to secure the future. This budget ensures the U.S.
Army can compete and win, now and for decades to come.

                                                               THIS WE'LL DEFEND; BE ALL YOU CAN BE!




                                                                                                           Exhibit PBA-19 Introductory Statement (Appropriation Highlights)

Mandatory Funding Justification

The FY 2027 request for Operation and Maintenance, Army (OMA) includes $64,114,683 thousand of discretionary and $9,464,115 thousand of mandatory for a total of
$73,578,798 thousand. The mandatory funds $4,077,673 thousand of facility sustainment, restoration, and modernization (FSRM) to improve barracks facilities across the
Army. It also funds $5,386,442 in FSRM, base operations support (BOS), and engineering support to mitigate poor and failing infrastructure. Funding will provide an
enhanced level of sustainment for facilities, as well as prioritizing the restoration and modernization of unaccompanied housing, and other critical facilities across Army
installations, including the organic industrial base. This funding is necessary to provide quality housing and operational facilities to Army Soldiers and civilians. Funding
improves Quality-of-Life and enhances the Warrior Ethos by investing in the readiness of our warfighting facilities and infrastructure.

The FY 2026 spend plan amount for OMA includes $57,535,277 thousand in discretionary and $2,905,190 thousand in mandatory for a total of $60,440,467 thousand.

The FY 2025 spend plan amount for OMA includes $62,402,003 thousand in discretionary and $147,700 thousand in mandatory for a total of $62,549,703 thousand.

 Budget Activity                                                  Actuals         Change          Change           Enacted         Change          Change           Request

 Operating Forces (BA-01)                                         41,409.5            649.1         -3,487.8       38,570.7            845.2         3,253.6        42,669.5

Budget Activity 01: Operating Forces - Major Program Changes:

The Operating Forces budget activity is crucial for sustaining the daily operations of our ground and air units, installations, and the dedicated Soldiers ensuring the
readiness of our combat elements. This budget activity guarantees the operational prowess of Army forces and consists of five activity groups: Land Forces, Land Forces
Readiness, Land Forces Readiness Support, Combatant Command Support, and Cyber Activities.

Land Forces provides for the resourcing of the ground and air Operating Tempo training for all Brigade Combat Teams (BCTs), modular support brigades, echelons above
brigade, theater level assets, land forces operations support, aviation assets, and special operations forces.

Land Forces Readiness activity supports critical elements such as training support centers, ranges, and operations, ensuring our forces are prepared for the challenges
ahead. It also focuses on active component support to reserve components, combat development testing, and force structure development. Additionally, this activity group
includes the Army’s Depot Maintenance program and Medical Readiness.

Land Forces Readiness Support provides for the Active Army's Installation services worldwide, fostering an environment in which Soldiers, Civilians and Families can
thrive, and provides a structure that supports an expeditionary Army with information and community services, municipal services, logistics, and security; maintains and
sustains the Army's facilities, restores facilities to industry standards, and modernizes facilities to meet the full range of tasks necessary to provide relevant and ready land
power for this Nation.

Combatant Command Support funds the headquarters core and direct missions for the four Commands for which Army is the Combatant Command Support Agency
(CCSA).

Cyber Activities provide vital capabilities to secure freedom of maneuver in cyberspace, ensuring we accomplish objectives, deny adversaries freedom of action, and
counter insider threats.

Primary Combat Formations: The budget supports 11 Armored Brigade Combat Teams (BCTs), 14 Mobile Brigade Combat Teams, 6 Stryker Brigade Combat Teams
(total of 31 Active Component BCTs), 2 Security Force Assistance Brigades, and 11 Combat Aviation Brigades. In FY 2027 the Army continues to develop and exercise
Multi-Domain capabilities and posture in the Indo-Pacific Theater in support of competition against the pacing threat. Global Force Management remains a cornerstone,
allowing for active and scalable Joint Force employment to meet operational demand, be it competitive or contingency. FY 2027 accounts for force structure changes
through Continuous Transformation.

After accounting for inflationary price growth of $845 million there is an overall increase to programs of $3,254 million. Major program changes include:

                                                                       DEPARTMENT OF THE ARMY

                                                              Introductory Statement (Appropriation Highlights)
                                                                                 ($ In Millions)



Strengthening Deterrence and Global Campaigning. In FY 2027, the Army provides significant increased funding for Pacific Deterrence Initiative (PDI), a portion of which
is due to transfers into OMA from the Military Construction, Army and the Research, Development, Test, & Evaluation, Army appropriations. These transfers provide
installation services and infrastructure support for Kwajalein Atoll. This funding, along with sustained resources for operations in the U.S. CENTCOM and U.S. EUCOM
areas of responsibility, enables persistent campaigning, enhances interoperability with allies, and provides a credible deterrent force.

Resourcing Army Transformation: Continuous Transformation and force structure changes result in both strategic realignments and investments in new capabilities,
including Counter-Small Unmanned Aircraft Systems (C-sUAS) Batteries, Reconnaissance Companies for Mobile Brigade Combat Teams (MBCTs), force structure for the
Multi-Domain Task Force (MDTF), Field Artillery/Air Defense Artillery evolution, and Engineer reorganization. Funding is reduced for Gray Eagle operations and
sustainment, and through the inactivation of select Combat Service Support Battalions, Engineer Battalions, a Signal Company, and Mobile Protected Firepower (MPF)
detachments, and the conversion of a Combat Aviation Brigade.

Home Station Training / Ground Operations. This strategic request solidifies our resolve to rebuild core warfighting competencies and enhance our ability to deploy, fight,
sustain, and triumph against diverse threats. The FY 2027 budget funds ground unit Operating Tempo (OPTEMPO) at 1,299 Composite Training Miles. The budget
provides resources to train and sustain the active component combat forces at the highest training readiness levels consistent with mission requirements and supports the
active component ground OPTEMPO metrics, encompassing actual miles driven for home station training and Combat Training Center (CTC) rotations, and virtual miles
driven by using simulators such as the Close Combat Tactical Trainer and the Unit Conduct of Fire Trainer. As informed through the Global Force Management Allocation
Plan (GFMAP) and projected force structure adjustments, there are net overall increases to home station training.

There is home station training / growth throughout OPTEMPO SAGs for combat and tactical vehicle repair parts and in support of increased Army end strength.

This request maintains 22 BCT-level CTC rotations and maintains the Active/National Guard mix as in FY 2026. The CTC program does have reductions due to one-time
FY 2026 modernization requirements.

Air Operations / Flying Hour Program: The FY 2027 budget funds 166,000 Flying Hours (10.3 hours per crew per month), which is an increase from FY 2026 (8.2 H/C/M),
and provides sufficient resources to train and sustain Combat Aviation Brigades at required readiness levels to support GFMAP requirements.

Depot Maintenance: The FY 2027 request includes a significant increase to the depot maintenance program. This growth primarily restores discretionary funding for
requirements that were covered by mandatory funding in FY 2026. The program also includes real growth for Combat Vehicles, Next Generation Command and Control
systems, and Operational Readiness Program, partially offset by a decrease for Aviation based on induction forecasts.

Installation Readiness and Services: The Army's FY 2027 strategy for base operating services directs funding toward Life, Health, and Safety programs, ensuring our
Soldiers are not only trained but equipped to meet the demands of our nation. The Base Operation Support (BOS) funding request aligns seamlessly with recent execution
levels, assuring known requirements are adequately funded in 2027. BOS stays relatively flat overall, including increases to support mitigation of poor and failing facilities,
utilities, and environmental programs; increases are partially offset by decreases for facility operations, information technology (IT) services management, and logistic
operations, but BOS overall remains funded to 95% of requirements. Within Facility Sustainment, Restoration, and Modernization the Army continues to prioritize the

modernization of barracks with significant investments. Mandatory funding, not covered in this volume, provides additional funding for barracks improvements and to
mitigate poor and failing facilities. The discretionary budget funds facility sustainment to 65 percent of the Department of War Facility Sustainment Model. Mandatory
funding brings the facility sustainment funding to 91 percent.

Combatant Commands: This budget request directly resources the day-to-day activities and forward presence of Army forces assigned to Geographic Combatant
Commands (GCC). OMA funding is the primary enabler for persistent campaigning, allowing Army units to participate in joint and multinational exercises, security
cooperation events, and partnership-building activities. These engagements increase interoperability with our allies and partners, enhance regional stability, and signal
U.S. commitment and resolve, which are foundational to integrated deterrence. This request includes significant funding increases to resource these priorities, the details
of which are described within the appropriate sections of this volume.

Efficiencies within BA 01 total to $-259 million for contract services, supplies and materials, travel, and civilian workforce optimization.

 Budget Activity                                                   Actuals          Change           Change           Enacted          Change          Change           Request

 Mobilization (BA-02)                                                1,069.0             -44.6          -555.5            468.9             12.7           700.9          1,182.5

Budget Activity 02: Mobilization - Major Program Changes:

The mobilization budget activity stands as a critical pillar, offering immediate capabilities for deploying forces. It consists of three activities: Strategic Mobility, Army
Prepositioned Stocks (APS), and Industrial Preparedness.

Strategic Mobility: Facilitates the swift deployment of forces when needed, ensuring agility and precision in response to emerging challenges.

Army Prepositioned Stocks (APS): A strategic collection of globally positioned assets afloat and ashore to meet surges in military requirements at the onset of conflict.

Industrial Preparedness: Systematic analysis of the industrial base to identify and mitigate potential shortfalls, acting as a strategic hedge against uncertainties.

At its core, these activities fuel the preparation of troops and supplies for war, weaving together the intricate threads of the Army's Power Projection efforts, material
readiness in peacetime, and a meticulous analysis of the industrial base to overcome shortfalls in industrial capacity.

After accounting for inflationary price change of $13 million, BA 02 grows significantly by $701 million. This increase is driven almost entirely by strategic investments in
the Army's global prepositioned stocks.

The FY 2027 OMA request makes major investments in the Total Army Power Projection Equipment (TAP2E) initiative. TAP2E enhances global deterrence and
responsiveness by positioning combat-ready equipment sets, medical sustainment stocks, and munitions in key strategic regions. The primary focus of this budget is
increasing posture in the Indo-Pacific, which directly aligns with the National Defense Strategy.

Establishing APS-8 (Indo-Pacific): This budget funds the establishment of Army Prepositioned Stocks-8 in the Southwest Indo-Pacific, including initial operating costs and
a Care of Supplies in Storage (COSIS) contract. This ensures a credible, combat-configured equipment set is immediately available to the Combatant Commander,
drastically reducing deployment timelines in a crisis.

Sustaining APS-2 (Europe): The request also increases funding for the comprehensive maintenance and care of existing stocks, such as APS-2 in Europe, through COSIS
contracts.

Together, these TAP2E investments ensure that forward-positioned assets are maintained at the highest state of readiness, providing a tangible deterrent to adversaries
and a rapid response capability for the Joint Force.

                                                                                                               Exhibit PBA-19 Introductory Statement (Appropriation Highlights)

 Budget Activity                                                  Actuals          Change          Change           Enacted         Change          Change           Request

 Training and Recruiting (BA-03)                                    6,033.3             99.2           -766.6         5,365.9            96.9            163.9         5,626.7

Budget Activity 03: Training and Recruiting - Major Program Changes:

The Training and Recruiting budget activity provides funds for accessing and training Soldiers and civilians required to man the Army. This budget activity consists of three
activity groups: Accession Training; Basic Skill and Advanced Training; and Recruiting, Other Training and Education.

Accession Training transforms civilian volunteers from private citizens into trained enlisted Soldiers and commissioned officers to meet force structure requirements.

Basic Skill and Advanced Training produces technically competent Soldiers, ensuring they meet and exceed demands of modern warfare.

Recruiting and Other Training and Education ensures the Army is able to recruit quality Soldiers and provide continuing education for Soldiers and Army civilians.

The institutional training base is the keystone of Army readiness. It develops technically and tactically proficient Soldiers and leaders who are prepared to navigate the
complexities of a joint, interagency, intergovernmental, and multinational environment.

Overall, after accounting for inflationary price growth of $97 million the programs increase moderately by $164 million. This growth is primarily directed at training more
Soldiers to support higher end strength and expanding the JROTC program, partially offset by efficiencies and strategic realignments.

Investing in People and Training:

Supporting Army End Strength: This budget directly supports Army end strength growth by funding increased student attendance at Advanced Individual Training (AIT),
particularly for critical Military Occupational Specialties (MOS). It also provides resources for the institutional training base (instructors and schoolhouse costs) to establish
new AIT courses and manage the overall higher student load.

Junior Reserve Officers' Training Corps (JROTC): Funding increases to allow for the expansion of 40 JROTC programs in FY 2027. This growth also reverses reductions
made in FY 2026, restoring funding for instructors, materials, and equipment.

===== PAGE 15 =====
                                                                        DEPARTMENT OF THE ARMY
                                                                    Fiscal Year (FY) 2027 Budget Estimates
                                                                       Operation and Maintenance, Army
                                                               Introductory Statement (Appropriation Highlights)
                                                                                  ($ In Millions)


Realignments and Efficiencies:

Recruiting and Advertising: Following a significant investment in FY 2026 to establish the new Army Recruiting Enterprise, the FY 2027 budget reflects a planned reduction
in advertising and research efforts, particularly within paid media. The Army will leverage data-driven strategies and a refined marketing approach to sustain recruiting
momentum.

MEPCOM Transfer: This budget activity reflects the transfer of the U.S. Military Entrance Processing Command (USMEPCOM) from the Army to the Defense Human
Resources Activity (DHRA), resulting in a corresponding funding reduction in OMA.

Efficiencies: Across BA-03, targeted efficiencies for contract services, printing, supplies, travel, and civilian workforce optimization generate savings of $166 million.

                                                                 FY 2025          Price          Program          FY 2026         Price          Program         FY 2027
 Budget Activity                                                 Actuals         Change          Change           Enacted        Change          Change          Request

 Administration and Servicewide Activities (BA-04)               13,890.3            249.9        -1,010.4        13,129.9          -183.2         1,689.4        14,636.0

Budget Activity 04: Administration and Servicewide Activities - Major Program Changes:


The Administration and Servicewide Activities budget activity provides funds for the administration, logistics, communications, and other Servicewide support functions
required to support global operations and Army forces worldwide. This budget activity consists of four activity groups: Security Programs, Logistics Operations,
Servicewide Support, and Support of Other Nations.

Security Programs include classified and unclassified programs in support of Cryptology, Defense Intelligence, Foreign Counterintelligence, National Geospatial-
Intelligence, Military Intelligence Program, Security and Intelligence Activities, and Arms Control Treaties implementation and compliance.

Logistics Operations ensure the logistics for transportation, supplies, support activities, and ammunition management. Logistics Operations support requirements for force
modernization and equipment fielding and also support funding increases for demilitarization preparation and end-item disposal due to the Army's initiative to rapidly divest
excess equipment resulting from force structure reductions.

Servicewide Activities include those programs that impact the whole Army in administration, communications, manpower and talent management, service support by Joint
and Defense agencies, claims against the Army, real estate management, and Financial Improvement and Audit Readiness.

Support of Other Nations funds the Department of War's contribution to the North Atlantic Treaty Organization (NATO) and directed missions to other nations in support of
security cooperation strategies and stability with other nations.

Overall, after accounting for price changes of $-183 million, the programs in this budget activity increase by $1,689 million. This growth is driven by significant
investments in intelligence programs, strategic logistics, enterprise IT, and support to NATO.

Investing in Intelligence and Security:

Security Programs: The FY 2027 request includes significant growth to support Operational Aerial Intelligence, Surveillance, and Reconnaissance (ISR) for U.S. Africa
Command and U.S. Southern Command. The budget also increases funding for other intelligence activities, support systems, and networks.

Personnel Security: Funding grows to meet Triggered Enhanced Subject Interview (TESI) requirements and to support the continuous vetting of national security and low-
risk suitability populations, strengthening the integrity of the force.


                                                                                                           Exhibit PBA-19 Introductory Statement (Appropriation Highlights)

                                                                                      12

===== PAGE 17 =====
                                                                        DEPARTMENT OF THE ARMY
                                                                    Fiscal Year (FY) 2027 Budget Estimates
                                                                       Operation and Maintenance, Army
                                                               Introductory Statement (Appropriation Highlights)
                                                                                  ($ In Millions)


Enhancing Global Logistics:
This request makes key investments in logistics to support Army Continuous Transformation and global readiness. Growth in Second Destination Transportation is
required to move equipment as the force reorganizes. Additionally, there are significant increases for ammunition management to support the distribution, receipt of new
production, and storage of Joint Service conventional ammunition, ensuring sustained operational readiness for the Joint Force.

Modernizing Enterprise IT and Communications:
FY 2027 includes increases for Enterprise Identity, Credential, and Access Management, a cornerstone of the Army's adherence to Zero Trust security principles. Funding
is also increased for the comprehensive modernization of digital records and storage systems.

Investing in Strategic Capabilities and Alliances:

Artificial Intelligence (AI): The budget reflects significant growth to accelerate the integration and scaling of AI, with a specific focus on Project ARIA (Army Rapid
Implementation of Artificial Intelligence).

North Atlantic Treaty Organization (NATO): To demonstrate our commitment to our allies, this budget includes a significant funding increase for the NATO military budget
that meets NATO common funding commitments. The FY 2027 request fully funds the NATO Military Budget requirement ($756.7 million) as part of the United States'
common funding requirement.

Efficiencies: BA-04 accounts for $107 million in savings from efficiencies in contract services, supplies, travel, and civilian workforce optimization.

 Strategic Readiness                                             Actuals         Change          Change           Enacted        Change          Change          Request

 Total Strategic Readiness                                       24,817.2            368.2        -4,942.1        20,243.4           513.0         3,551.0        24,307.4

Description of Strategic Readiness Financed:


The Army's FY 2027 Operation and Maintenance (OMA) readiness budget is fundamental to achieving the objectives outlined in the 2026 National Defense Strategy
(NDS). This funding directly generates combat credible land power by focusing on two core defense priorities: Rebuilding the Military and Reestablishing Deterrence.

Readiness resources are laser-focused on building and sustaining proficiency in core warfighting tasks, from the individual Soldier to the Brigade Combat Team (BCT)
level and above. This budget underpins the Army's Continuous Transformation efforts by ensuring that as the force reorganizes and modernizes, it simultaneously
maintains the highest possible state of readiness.

The FY 2027 readiness request, a subset of BA-01, totals $24,307 million. After accounting for $513 million in price changes, this represents a net program increase of
$3,551 million. The following sections articulate how these readiness investments directly support these two key defense priorities.

Priority 1: Rebuild the Military

This priority focuses on restoring the foundational warfighting readiness of the force. OMA readiness funding rebuilds the military by resourcing the rigorous training, expert
maintenance, and critical support systems that generate lethal and resilient combat formations.

    •   Investing in Foundational Training: The request increases funding for Home Station Training, resourcing unit OPTEMPO at 1,299 Composite Training Miles. This
        provides the fuel, repair parts, and ammunition for units to achieve and sustain proficiency in their core warfighting tasks. It also funds the increased training
        requirements associated with growing the Army's end strength and the activation of new formations like c-UAS batteries, modernized ADA units, and transformed
        BCTs.

    •   Restoring Aviation Readiness: The budget significantly increases the Flying Hour Program to 166,000 hours (10.3 hrs/crew/month). This investment is critical to
        rebuilding the readiness of our Combat Aviation Brigades, ensuring they are prepared to meet global force projection requirements.

    •   Improving Equipment Readiness: This request makes a substantial investment in Depot Maintenance, which includes restoring discretionary funding that was
        covered by a one-time mandatory provision in FY 2026. This funding directly reduces the backlog of non-mission capable equipment, overhauls combat vehicles
        and command-and-control systems, and ensures our formations have the combat-ready equipment necessary for training and deployment.


                                                                                                           Exhibit PBA-19 Introductory Statement (Appropriation Highlights)

                                                                                      14

===== PAGE 19 =====
                                                                       DEPARTMENT OF THE ARMY
                                                                   Fiscal Year (FY) 2027 Budget Estimates
                                                                      Operation and Maintenance, Army
                                                              Introductory Statement (Appropriation Highlights)
                                                                                 ($ In Millions)


    •   Modernizing Training Systems: The budget increases funding for Training Support Systems (TSS) and Commercial Satellite Air Time for Next Generation
        Command and Control (NGC2). These investments ensure our Soldiers and leaders can train using modern systems and are prepared to fight and win in a
        contested, information-degraded environment.

Priority 2: Reestablish Deterrence

This priority focuses on projecting credible combat strength to discourage aggression and assure our partners. OMA readiness funding reestablishes deterrence by
enabling the Army to campaign globally, demonstrate capability in priority theaters, and support joint, interagency, and multinational operations.

    •   Campaigning in the Indo-Pacific: The budget increases funding for the Pacific Deterrence Initiative (PDI), specifically for joint and multinational exercises. These
        events allow U.S. Army forces to train alongside key allies, build interoperability and trust, and signal an unambiguous commitment to regional security, which is
        the cornerstone of integrated deterrence.

    •   Supporting Homeland Defense: The request funds the readiness of units assigned to support the U.S. southwest border security mission. By providing resources
        for this critical homeland defense operation, the Army demonstrates its capacity to secure the nation while simultaneously projecting strength abroad.

Collectively, these readiness investments ensure the Army is resourced to meet its global mission requirements and remains fully aligned with the strategic imperatives of
the National Defense Strategy. Ultimately, this request builds and sustains a more lethal, responsive, and resilient land force for the Nation.


The FY 2027 request for Readiness includes $24,307,447 thousand of discretionary and $0 thousand of mandatory (reconciliation) for a total of $24,307,447 thousand to
directly support warfighters' readiness.

                                                                   Budget Activity 01: Operating Forces
                                                                      Activity Group 11: Land Forces
                                                             Detail by Subactivity Group 111: Maneuver Units

I. Description of Operations Financed:
MANEUVER UNITS - Funds the training and operations required to maintain readiness in the active Army's Brigade Combat Teams (BCT) and all organic forces
associated with those BCTs through force generation, while leveraging live, virtual, and constructive capabilities to conduct training focused on multidomain operations.
Funds training for units available to train at home station. Includes mission support across multiple theaters of operations.

The Army resources all active component ground units to conduct multidomain operations training and achieve the highest training readiness levels attainable based on
available resources. The Army remains committed to providing the necessary resources to train and prepare Soldiers, leaders, and units to support current and future
operations.

HOME STATION TRAINING - Funds unit training at home station and includes the operation and maintenance of unit ground equipment.

MANDATORY FUNDING JUSTIFICATION:
The FY 2026 spend plan amount for SAG 111 includes $4,401,507 thousand of discretionary and $361,254 thousand of mandatory for a total of $4,762,761 thousand.


II. Force Structure Summary:
The Maneuver Unit force structure reflects the Active Army's structure at the Brigade Combat Team (BCT) level and below. This Subactivity Group (SAG) funds the
Division headquarters, Armored BCTs, Mobile BCTs (includes airborne and air assault BCTs), Stryker BCTs, and Security Force Assistance Brigades. In addition, this
SAG funds operation and maintenance of authorized equipment such as tanks, armored personnel carriers, Stryker armored vehicles, tactical wheeled vehicles, radios,
and reconnaissance and surveillance platforms. Also, funds operation and maintenance of Army common equipment for Special Forces Groups and the 75th Ranger
Regiment.


Army Service Component Commands:
U.S. Army Western Hemisphere Command
U.S. Army Pacific
U.S. Army Europe and Africa
U.S. Army Central
U.S. Army Special Operations Command

Direct Reporting Units (DRUs):
U.S. Army Acquisition Support Center

          a) Annualization of New FY 2026 Program .................................................................................................................................................................................... $0

          b) One-Time FY 2027 Costs ............................................................................................................................................................................................... $100,888

                      1) Brigade Combat Team (BCT) Conversion - Continuous Transformation ............................................................................................. $100,888
                      Increases funding for the maintenance, repair, and care of supplies in storage for 87 M1A2 Abrams tanks to meet the Army's fully mission
                      capable standard prior to being fielded to the 3rd Cavalry Regiment which converts to an Armored Brigade Combat Team in FY 2028.
                      (Baseline: $4,401,507)

          c) Program Growth in FY 2027 ........................................................................................................................................................................................... $900,650

                      1) Home Station Training - Critical Repair Parts ....................................................................................................................................... $105,151
                      Increases funding for sustainment costs for combat and tactical vehicles in the BCT fleets to address critical repair parts backlog. Fleets
                      affected are the M1 Tank, M2 Bradley Fighting Vehicle, M113 Carriers, M88 Recovery Vehicles, Heavy Expanded Mobility Tactical Trucks
                      (HEMTT), Medium Tactical Vehicles (MTV) and Joint Light Tactical Vehicles (JLTV). (Baseline: $2,361,983)

                      2) Home Station Training - End Strength Growth ..................................................................................................................................... $178,493
                      Increases funding for the Army’s end strength growth to deliver ready combat forces to the Joint Force. Funding enhances readiness by
                      increasing Home Station Training funding for individual and collective training. (Baseline: $2,361,983)

                                                                        DEPARTMENT OF THE ARMY
                                                                   Fiscal Year (FY) 2027 Budget Estimates
                                                                       Operation and Maintenance, Army
                                                                     Budget Activity 01: Operating Forces
                                                                        Activity Group 11: Land Forces
                                                               Detail by Subactivity Group 111: Maneuver Units


3) Home Station Training - Korea Brigade Combat Team (BCT) Rotation ................................................................................................. $20,674
Increases funding supporting a BCT on nine-month cyclical rotations to Korea in support of current Military Strategy. Cost increase is due to
two transfers of authority (TOAs) taking place in FY 2027 vice one in FY 2026. The nine-month rotation cycle generates two TOAs in the
same FY every two years. The funding increase supports unit personnel transportation costs to and from Korea and increases in equipment
transportation costs. This Stryker Brigade Combat Team (SBCT) rotation supports the Indo-Pacific region and strengthens alliance with the
Republic of Korea. (Baseline: $2,361,983)

4) Home Station Training - Military Intelligence (MI) Force Structure ........................................................................................................... $2,908
Increases funding for units’ home station training costs due to Army Force Structure activations for BCT MI Companies. Army activates two
SBCT MI Companies, one in Europe and one in Joint Base Lewis-McChord, Washington. Funds repair parts, fuel, supplies, and other home
station training support, to include transportation costs. (Baseline: $2,361,983)

5) Home Station Training - Signal Force Structure ....................................................................................................................................... $8,407
Increases funding for units’ home station training costs due to restructuring of signal units supporting Brigade Combat Teams (BCT) and
Divisions. Army activates four Division Signal Battalions and two SBCT signal companies as well as inactivating two Mobile Brigade Combat
Team (MBCT) signal companies. Funds repair parts, fuel, supplies, and other home station training support, to include transportation costs.
(Baseline: $2,361,983)

6) Home Station Training - Training Readiness ........................................................................................................................................ $180,986
Increases funding for Home Station Training (HST) which focuses on warfighting readiness as the Army continues to restore core capabilities
with all units achieving the highest levels of training readiness attainable. Increases funding for BCT sustainment costs for repair parts, fuel,
supplies, and other home station training support costs commensurate with increased training. (Baseline: $2,361,983)

7) Homeland Border Security - National Defense Areas .......................................................................................................................... $254,444
Increases funding for the deployment, training, and equipping of units mobilized to operationalize and proactively deter threats to National
Defense Areas in accordance with Executive Order 14167, Clarifying the Military's Role in Protecting the Territorial Integrity of the United
States. (Baseline: $0)

8) Pacific Deterrence Initiative (PDI) - Exercises, Training, and Experimentation ........................................................................................ $3,508
Increases funding for campaigning in the Indo-Pacific. Funds travel, transportation, supplies, and equipment maintenance by contract for
units. This increases the Army's investment and focus on campaigning in the Indo-Pacific theater and the Pacific Deterrence Initiative.
(Baseline: $20,320)

                     10) Strategic Lift and Transportation ......................................................................................................................................................... $132,405
                     Increases funding to maintain the land, air, and sea transportation for deployment/redeployment and intra-theater movements for troops and
                     equipment in the USCENTCOM area of responsibility to meet approved Global Force Management Allocation Plan (GFMAP) rotations.
                     Funding addresses consistent shortfalls in strategic lift. (Baseline: $178,415)

NOTES:
1. The Army began converting the IBCTs to MBCTs in FY 2025 to improve mobility and lethality in a leaner formation.
2. Composite Miles are calculated based on the Brigade Combat Team (BCT) force structure, BCTs available for home
station training and Force Generation training requirements. Composite Miles includes the M1 and M2 in the Armored BCT;
Up-Armored High Mobility Multipurpose Wheeled Vehicle/Infantry Squad Vehicle in the Mobile BCT infantry battalions;
and all Strykers in the Stryker infantry battalions.
3. Composite Miles measure funded levels of training and applies to all units in Activity Group 11: Land Forces.
4. Funds the Directed Readiness Table Requirements in FY 2025-2027.

                                                        Detail by Subactivity Group 112: Modular Support Brigades

I. Description of Operations Financed:
MODULAR SUPPORT BRIGADES - Funds the training and operations of multi-functional support brigades through force generation, while leveraging live, virtual, and
constructive capabilities to conduct training focused on multidomain operations. Funds training for units available to train at home station.

The Army resources all active component ground units to conduct multidomain operations and achieve the highest training readiness levels attainable based on available
resources. The Army remains committed to providing the necessary resources to train and prepare Soldiers, leaders, and units to support current and future operations.

HOME STATION TRAINING - Funds unit training at home station and includes the operation and maintenance of unit ground equipment.



II. Force Structure Summary:
The Modular Support Brigades force structure reflects the Active Army's Multi-Functional Support Brigades such as Fires, Expeditionary Military Intelligence, and
Sustainment Brigades that support the Brigade Combat Teams. Includes the Multi-Domain Task Force in SAG 112 force structure.

Army Service Component Commands:
U.S. Army Western Hemisphere Command
U.S. Army Pacific
U.S. Army Europe and Africa

c) Program Growth in FY 2027 ........................................................................................................................................................................................... $143,476

           1) Home Station Training - Critical Repair Parts ......................................................................................................................................... $45,806
           Increases funding for sustainment costs for combat and tactical vehicles in the Multifunctional Support Brigade fleets to address critical repair
           parts backlog. Fleets affected are the Multiple Launch Rocket System (MLRS), Heavy Expanded Mobility Tactical Trucks (HEMTT), Medium
           Tactical Vehicles (MTV) and Up-armored High Mobility Multipurpose Wheeled Vehicles (HMMWV). (Baseline: $219,978)

           2) Home Station Training - End Strength Growth ....................................................................................................................................... $23,356
           Increases funding for the Army’s end strength growth to deliver ready combat forces to the Joint Force. Funding enhances readiness by
           increasing Home Station Training funding for individual and collective training. (Baseline: $219,978)

           3) Home Station Training - Field Artillery Force Structure ............................................................................................................................ $3,772
           Increases funding for home station training costs for a new High Mobility Artillery Rocket System (HIMARS) Battalion and its associated
           maintenance support company in Schofield Barracks, Hawaii. Funds repair parts, fuel, supplies, and other home station training support costs
           commensurate with unit training missions. (Baseline: $219,978)

           4) Home Station Training - Multi-Domain Task Force (MDTF) Force Structure ......................................................................................... $34,743
           Increases funding for home station training costs for three converted Multi-Domain Effects Battalion (MDEB), the activations of the
           4th MDTF and one Multi-Domain Command (MDC). Army completes the growth of MDEBs to full MDTF strength for 1st, 2nd, and
           3rd MDTFs. Additionally, the Army activates the 4th MDTF in Fort Carson, Colorado with one MDEB, one Strategic Fires Battalion
           with associated Forward Support Company and the MDTF Support Company. Additionally, the Army activates MDC-Army at Fort
           Bragg, North Carolina. Funds repair parts, fuel, supplies, and other home station training support costs commensurate with unit
           training missions. (Baseline: $219,978)

                     5) Home Station Training - Training Readiness .......................................................................................................................................... $35,799
                     Increases funding for Home Station Training (HST) which focuses on warfighting readiness as the Army continues to restore core capabilities
                     with all units achieving the highest levels of training readiness attainable. Increases funding for Multifunctional Support Brigades’ sustainment
                     costs for repair parts, fuel, supplies, and other home station training support costs commensurate with increased training. (Baseline:
                     $219,978)

                                                          Detail by Subactivity Group 113: Echelons Above Brigade

I. Description of Operations Financed:
ECHELONS ABOVE BRIGADE - Funds the training and operations of echelons above brigade operational units whose mission is to provide critical, actionable
intelligence, force protection, and area logistics support to Brigade Combat Teams through force generation, while leveraging live, virtual, and constructive capabilities to
conduct training focused on multidomain operations. Funds training for units available to train at home station. This includes critical tactical and national assets, such as:
Phased Array Tracking Radar to Intercept on Target Air Defense Battalions and Chemical, Biological, Radiological/Nuclear, and Explosive units/operations required to
protect both deployed units and the homeland. It also funds Army support to military police units providing force protection.

The Army resources all active component ground units to conduct multidomain operations training and achieve the highest training readiness levels attainable based on
available resources. The Army remains committed to providing the necessary resources to train and prepare Soldiers, leaders, and units to support current and future
operations.

HOME STATION TRAINING - Funds unit training at home station and includes the operation and maintenance of unit ground equipment.

II. Force Structure Summary:
Echelons Above Brigade force structure includes chemical, engineer, medical, signal, military police, military intelligence, air defense artillery, and logistics units. Some of
these units are aligned with the various multi-functional and functional support brigades in Subactivity Groups 112 Modular Support Brigades and 114 Theater Level
Assets. In addition, funds all of the authorized equipment such as Stryker; armored personnel carriers; Patriot missile launchers; Avenger air defense weapons; wheeled
vehicles; radios; and reconnaissance and surveillance platforms.

Headquarters, Department of the Army

Army Commands:
U.S. Army Transformation and Training Command
U.S. Army Materiel Command

Army Service Component Commands:
U.S. Army Western Hemisphere Command
U.S. Army Pacific

U.S. Army Cyber Command

U.S. Army Intelligence and Security Command

         c) Program Growth in FY 2027 ........................................................................................................................................................................................... $267,009

                     1) Home Station Training - Air Defense Artillery (ADA) Force Structure .................................................................................................... $26,403
                     Increases funding for home station training for two converted ADA battalions and one new Counter-Small Unmanned Aircraft Systems (C-
                     sUAS) ADA Battery. One Indirect Fire Protection Capability (IFPC) ADA Battalion converts to a composite IFPC & C-sUAS ADA Battalion
                     supporting the 1st Multi-Domain Task Force (MDTF) and the other Maneuver Short Range Air Defense (M-SHORAD) Battalion converts to a
                     Division ADA Battalion (DivAD). Funding provides repair parts, fuel, supplies, and other home station training support, to include
                     transportation costs. (Baseline: $877,892)

                     2) Home Station Training - Combat Service Support (CSS) Force Structure ............................................................................................. $10,500
                     Increases funding for home station training for one new Composite Watercraft Company and three new Explosive Ordnance Detachments
                     (EOD). The composite watercraft company contains five Landing Craft Utility (LCU), four Landing Craft Mechanized (LCM), and two
                     Tugboats. Funds repair parts, fuel, supplies, and other home station training support costs commensurate with unit mission. (Baseline:
                     $877,892)

                     3) Home Station Training - Critical Repair Parts ......................................................................................................................................... $85,431
                     Increases funding for sustainment costs for combat and tactical vehicles in the Functional Support Brigade fleets to address critical repair
                     parts backlog. Fleets affected are the M2 Bradley Fighting Vehicle, Assault Bridges, Heavy Expanded Mobility Tactical Trucks (HEMTT), and
                     Medium Tactical Vehicles (MTV). (Baseline: $877,892)

                     4) Home Station Training - End Strength Growth ....................................................................................................................................... $37,277
                     Increases funding for the Army’s end strength growth to deliver ready combat forces to the Joint Force. Funding enhances readiness by
                     increasing Home Station Training funding for individual and collective training. (Baseline: $877,892)

                     5) Home Station Training - Engineer Force Structure................................................................................................................................. $25,172
                     Increases funding for units’ home station training costs due to reorganization of engineer units and equipment updates to some
                     engineer formations. The Army Reorganized several Engineer formations to include realignment of Engineer Brigade and Battalion
                     Headquarters, activation of Combat Engineer Companies-Armored (CEC-A) and multirole bridge companies and inactivation of
                     engineer Clearance Companies. Additionally, the Army updated major equipment for two CEC-A’s from M113 Troop Carriers to M2
                     Bradley Fighting Vehicles, aligning with other CEC-A major equipment sets. This reorganization enables Armored and Mobile
                     Brigade Combat Team (BCT) formations and Division operations. Funds repair parts, fuel, supplies, and other home station training
                     support, to include transportation costs. (Baseline: $877,892)

                     6) Home Station Training - Training Readiness .......................................................................................................................................... $82,226
                     Increases funding for Home Station Training (HST) which focuses on warfighting readiness as the Army continues to restore core capabilities
                     with all units achieving the highest levels of training readiness attainable. Increases funding for Echelons Above Brigades’ sustainment costs
                     for repair parts, fuel, supplies, and other home station training support costs commensurate with increased training. (Baseline: $877,892)

I. Description of Operations Financed:
THEATER LEVEL ASSETS - Funds the training and operations of military units that directly support world-wide operations, the deployable elements of the Army Service
Component Commands headquarters, through force generation, while leveraging live, virtual, and constructive capabilities to conduct training focused on multidomain
operations. Funds home station training for units available. Supports world-wide information operations and actionable intelligence to include reach back capability for
deployed forces. Includes support for equipment, facilities, and all associated costs specifically identified to these units. Includes mission support across multiple theaters
of operations.

The Army resources all active component ground units to conduct multidomain operations training and achieve the highest training readiness levels attainable based on
available resources. The Army remains committed to providing the necessary resources to train and prepare Soldiers, leaders, and units to support current and future
operations.

HOME STATION TRAINING - Funds unit training at home station and includes the operation and maintenance of unit ground equipment.

BALKANS - Funds the incremental cost of operations in Bosnia and Kosovo. Supports training man-days for U.S. support to North Atlantic Treaty Organization
Headquarters Sarajevo (Bosnia) and Kosovo rotations.

ARMY EXERCISES - Funds Army Service Component Command exercises and Army participation in combatant command exercises. Includes funding the development,
planning, and execution of Army exercises.

MANDATORY FUNDING JUSTIFICATION:
The FY 2026 spend plan amount for SAG 114 includes $2,195,196 thousand of discretionary and $100,000 thousand of mandatory for a total of $2,295,196 thousand.


II. Force Structure Summary:
Theater Level Assets are the Active Army's functional support brigade headquarters and subordinate Army commands such as signal, medical, and financial
management. Structure includes deployable command posts of the Army Service Component Commands, theater information advantage detachments, criminal
investigation units, logistics units, and air defense/air space mission command units. Although called theater level assets, these units support combat forces throughout
the world and provide critical reach back capability in the areas of information operations, intelligence, and logistics.

Headquarters, Department of the Army

Army Commands:
U.S. Army Materiel Command

Army Service Component Commands:
U.S. Army Western Hemisphere Command

                     1) Army Exercises ............................................................................................................................................................................................ $970
                     Increases funding for Army exercises in the U.S. Army Central theater. Funds transportation, travel, supplies and materials, and contracts for
                     participating units. (Baseline: $16,522)

                     2) Balkans ..................................................................................................................................................................................................... $1,654
                     Increases funding for transportation, contracts, and supplies supporting the Mission Rehearsal Exercise (MRE) for rotational forces
                     conducting the Balkans peace keeping operations. (Baseline: $68,283)

                     3) Civilian Average Salary Adjustments ........................................................................................................................................................... $262
                     Adjusts funding due to changes to civilian compensation rates and civilian type composition within this SAG. Each year the Army uses
                     detailed execution and cost factor analysis to develop civilian rates for the budget request. (Baseline: $23,070)

                     4) Enhanced Performance Award Pool for DoW Civilians ............................................................................................................................... $462
                     Increases funding to strengthen the Department’s ability to recognize and reward the civilian workforce whose expertise and continuity
                     underpin mission success. Expanded award capacity helps retain the talent that sustains warfighter readiness, drives innovation, and ensures
                     uninterrupted support across the enterprise. Investing in civilian recognition directly reinforces the workforce that delivers stability, operational
                     excellence, and long-term capability for the Department. (Baseline: $23,070)

5) Home Station Training - Air Defense Artillery (ADA) Force Structure .................................................................................................... $13,374
Increases funding for home station training for one new Composite ADA Battalion (PATRIOT & IFPC) in the Indo-Pacific Area of
Responsibility (AOR). Funding provides repair parts, fuel, supplies, and other home station training support, to include transportation costs.
(Baseline: $738,679)

6) Home Station Training - Chemical Defense Equipment ......................................................................................................................... $33,750
Increases funding for the procurement and sustainment of additional Joint Service Lightweight Integrated Suit Technology (JSLIST) suits.
This supports an increased JSLIST requirement, critical to the protection against chemical, biological, radiological, and nuclear threats. The
Army will continue to prioritize resources to JSLIST suits. (Baseline: $738,679)

7) Home Station Training - Counter-Small Unmanned Aircraft Systems (C-sUAS) Batteries .................................................................... $96,408
Increases funding to provide sustainment capabilities as the Army fields C-sUAS Batteries. These batteries are equipped with systems
designed to detect, track, and neutralize sUAS threats through multiple technologies. The integration with Maneuver Short Range Air Defense
(MSHORAD) systems ensures maneuver forces are protected against both traditional aerial threats and the growing use of drones. Funding
provides contract maintenance support for these units. (Baseline: $738,679)

8) Home Station Training - Critical Repair Parts ......................................................................................................................................... $19,403
Increases funding for sustainment costs for combat and tactical vehicles in the Theater Functional Support Brigade fleets to address critical
repair parts backlog. Fleets affected are the Heavy Expanded Mobility Tactical Trucks (HEMTT), Medium Tactical Vehicles (MTV) and Up-
armored High Mobility Multipurpose Wheeled Vehicles (HMMWV). (Baseline: $738,679)

9) Home Station Training - End Strength Growth ....................................................................................................................................... $26,146
Increases funding for the Army’s end strength growth to deliver ready combat forces to the Joint Force. Funding enhances readiness by
increasing Home Station Training funding for individual and collective training. (Baseline: $738,679)

10) Home Station Training - Theater Strike Effects Group (TSEG) Force Structure .................................................................................... $1,416
Funds home station training for one new TSEG Headquarters Company in the Pacific Theater. TSEG is forward stationed with a Theater
Army to provide dedicated and persistent space capabilities in the Geographic Combatant Commander's (GCC) area of responsibility (AOR).
Funds repair parts, fuel, supplies, and other home station training support costs commensurate with unit mission. (Baseline: $738,679)

                     11) Home Station Training - Training Readiness ........................................................................................................................................ $38,534
                     Increases funding for Home Station Training (HST) which focuses on warfighting readiness as the Army continues to restore core capabilities
                     with all units achieving the highest levels of training readiness attainable. Increases funding for Theater Level Assets’ sustainment costs for
                     repair parts, fuel, supplies, and other home station training support costs commensurate with increased training. (Baseline: $738,679)

                     12) Pacific Deterrence Initiative (PDI) - Exercises, Training, and Experimentation .................................................................................. $269,035
                     Increases funding for Army exercises and operations in the Indo-Pacific. Funds transportation, travel, supplies and materials, and contracts.
                     This increases the Army's investment and focus on exercises and operations in the Indo-Pacific theater and the Pacific Deterrence Initiative.
                     (Baseline: $321,727)

                     13) United States Central Command - Operations .......................................................................................................................................... $490
                     Increases funding to sustain U.S. military operations, force protection, and deterrence in the United States Central Command (USCENTCOM)
                     area of responsibility. Funding supports fuel, supplies, equipment and repair parts for unit operations. (Baseline: $56,066)

                     14) Workforce Optimization - Refinements ...................................................................................................................................................... $838
                     Implements zero-sum adjustments to the FY 2026 workforce optimization reductions. Army commands and organizations are correcting
                     reductions by exception due to the short time frame for implementing the FY 2026 reductions. (Baseline: $23,070; 7 FTE)

                     Decreases funding for exercises in Europe to align with the National Defense Strategy. Funding decreases for transportation and contracts
                     associated with the planning and execution of exercises. (Baseline: $55,911)

                                                                 Budget Activity 01: Operating Forces
                                                                    Activity Group 11: Land Forces
                                                   Detail by Subactivity Group 115: Land Forces Operations Support

I. Description of Operations Financed:
LAND FORCES OPERATIONS SUPPORT - Funds force related maneuver training and warfighter training exercises at the National Training Center, the Joint Readiness
Training Center, the Joint Multinational Readiness Center, the Joint Pacific Multinational Readiness Center, and the U.S. Army Combined Arms Center (Mission Command
Training Program). Funds support joint training integration during Combat Training Center exercises. Also funds operation and maintenance support that includes
contractor logistics support for selected pieces of tactical unit equipment. Includes mission support across multiple theaters of operations.

COMBAT TRAINING CENTER (CTC) TRAINING PROGRAM - Funds force related maneuver and warfighter exercise training at the National Training Center, the Joint
Readiness Training Center, the Joint Multinational Readiness Center, the Joint Pacific Multinational Readiness Center and the Mission Command Training Program.
Funds support joint training integration during CTC exercises. Funding also supports CTC operations, ground Operating Tempo for the Opposing Force units, and the
deployment costs for the units training at the CTCs. Includes the operation and maintenance costs of the Observer Controller Groups and all other CTC elements that run
and support CTC operations. Funds force generation requirements for Special Forces Group Headquarters and above, brigade, division, corps, and Army Service
Component Command commanders and staffs by conducting computer simulation-driven exercises in a realistic, stressful, joint and combined arms training environment.
Funds exercises and simulations which are designed to support multidomain operations in a contemporary operational environment that include professional
Observer/Controller Observer Coach Trainers.

HOME STATION TRAINING - Funds operation and maintenance support that includes contractor logistics support of select equipment such as Aviation Aerial
Communications, Mission Command, Architecture and Environment Exploitation Systems, Biological Identification Detection Systems, Portal Shields, High Mobility Artillery
Rocket Systems, Multiple Launch Rocket Systems, Javelin and Tube-launched Optical-tracked Wire-guided Improved Target Acquisition Systems. Also funds Logistics
Readiness Centers and Brigade Safety Officers.



II. Force Structure Summary:
Combat Training Center locations:
Joint Multinational Readiness Center, Hohenfels, Germany
Joint Readiness Training Center, Fort Polk, Louisiana
National Training Center, Fort Irwin, California
Joint Pacific Multinational Readiness Center, Schofield Barracks, Hawaii
U.S. Army Combined Arms Center Mission Command Training Program, Fort Leavenworth, Kansas


Headquarters, Department of the Army

Army Commands:
U.S. Army Transformation and Training Command
U.S. Army Materiel Command

8. Program Increases ................................................................................................................................................................................................................................ $65,108

         a) Annualization of New FY 2026 Program .................................................................................................................................................................................... $0

         b) One-Time FY 2027 Costs .......................................................................................................................................................................................................... $0

         c) Program Growth in FY 2027 ............................................................................................................................................................................................. $65,108

                     1) Combat Training Center (CTC) Modernization ....................................................................................................................................... $32,915
                     Funds key initiatives at the CTCs and the Mission Command Training Program (MCTP) that support data actualization and analytics, enable
                     emerging threat replication during CTC rotations, and integration of Next Generation Constructive (NGC) simulations in Warfighter Exercises
                     (WFX). (Baseline: $1,057,617)

                     2) Combat Training Center (CTC) Rotations .............................................................................................................................................. $22,057
                     Funds increased costs for CTC rotations transportation and operations costs due to changes in Brigade Combat Team (BCT) equipment
                     authorizations and increased transportation rates as well as added operational costs due to change for one Active ABCT rotation replacing an
                     Active SBCT. (Baseline: $1,057,617)

                     3) Enhanced Performance Award Pool for DoW Civilians ............................................................................................................................ $2,583
                     Increases funding to strengthen the Department’s ability to recognize and reward the civilian workforce whose expertise and continuity
                     underpin mission success. Expanded award capacity helps retain the talent that sustains warfighter readiness, drives innovation, and ensures
                     uninterrupted support across the enterprise. Investing in civilian recognition directly reinforces the workforce that delivers stability, operational
                     excellence, and long-term capability for the Department. (Baseline: $162,892)
